It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. For example, we excluded purely science based classes from our review of Yale’s environment school, since most science based classes provided basic knowledge about problems – which might appear to be Type 4- but because they didn’t identify policy or governance solutions – where actually not part of our universe of analyses. **Article to Analyze:** Page 1: The limits and variety of risk-based governance: The case of ﬂood management in Germany and England Kristian Krieger War Studies Department, King’s College London, London, UK Abstract Risk-based governance is argued by many to hold the promise of a more rational and efﬁcient state, by making explicit the limitations of state interventions and focusing ﬁnite resources on those targets where probable damage is greatest. This paper challenges the assumption that risk-based governance has the potential for universal and uniform application, by comparing contemporary ﬂood management in Germany and England. On ﬁrst inspection, ﬂooding appears to be a para- digmatic case of risk colonizing European policy discourses, with the traditional notion of ﬂood defense giving way to ﬂood risk management in the context of climate change, increasingly frequent ﬂood disasters, political and cost pressures on ﬂood protection, and publicly available European- wide ﬂood assessments. Drawing on in-depth empirical research, this paper shows how the role, and even the deﬁnition, of “risk” is institutionally shaped, and how the respective institutional environments of German and English ﬂood management practices impede and promote risk colonization. In particular, the use and conceptualizations of risk in governance are variously promoted, ﬁltered, or constrained by the administrative procedures, structures, and political expec- tations embedded within ﬂood management and wider polities of each country. The ﬁndings of this research are important for the design and implementation of supranational policies and regulations that endorse risk-based approaches, such as the recent EU Flood Directive, as well as scholarly debate as to how to legitimately deﬁne the limits of governance in the face of uncertainty and accountability pressures. Keywords: accountability, comparative politics, ﬂood management, institutionalism, neoliberalism, risk-based governance. 1. Introduction This paper examines the following questions: Is risk-based governance the same every- where? If not, how does it vary? How can the variation be explained? The paper critically engages with the emerging scholarly debate about the dynamic rise of risk-based gover- nance across developed countries, and with wider questions of (institutional barriers to) the diffusion of policy concepts and approaches from one country to another. Risk-based governance implies that governance resources (e.g. ﬁnancial, regulatory, and informational measures) are allocated on the basis of calculations of risk (Rothstein Correspondence: Kristian Krieger, War Studies Department, King’s College London, London WC2R 2LS, UK. Email: kristian.1.krieger@kcl.ac.uk Accepted for publication 2 November 2012. bs_bs_banner Regulation & Governance (2013) 7, 236–257 doi:10.1111/rego.12009 © 2012 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd Page 2: et al. 2006b). Calculating risk normally means quantitatively assessing the probability of certain outcomes, for instance the economic losses from disasters. Increasingly, scholars have been making arguments about the “attractions of risk- based regulation” (Hutter 2005, p. 1; cf. Black & Baldwin 2012); the “risk colonization” of an increasing number of policy domains (Rothstein et al. 2006a, p. 91); the rise of “risk management of everything” (Power 2004, p. 1); and the pervasiveness of risk-based routines and practices of government in most areas of life (O’Malley 2004). This emerg- ing scholarly literature echoes the wide endorsement of the use of risk by policymakers: The European Commission calls risk a “crucial” component of public policy (EC 2002) while the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) recom- mends the wide adoption of risk-based approaches (OECD 2010). In these public and scholarly debates, the underlying drivers of this adoption are of two kinds, namely that risk-based governance ensures a more efﬁcient use of ﬁnite resources, as well as more accountable governance. In times when great attention is paid to the notions of austerity and good governance, arguments for the universal rise of risk-based governance, there- fore, seem to make good sense. Similar arguments can be made for the empirical case study of this paper, the management of ﬂooding. Flood management has attracted substantial attention from policymakers in European countries in recent years because of the dramatic increase in the number of ﬂoods causing signiﬁcant economic damage since the 1990s (Barredo 2007), including such disasters as the Elbe/Danube ﬂood in 2002, and the summer 2007 ﬂoods in England which caused about 11 billion US dollars and 4 billion US dollars in economic damage, respectively. In response to these disasters, ﬂood management underwent a paradigmatic shift across Europe from ﬂood defense to ﬂood risk management in the 1990s and 2000s (Mitchell 2003; Johnson et al. 2005). The predominant reliance on engineered ﬂood defenses (e.g. embankments and dykes) has increasingly been viewed as counterproduc- tive: Flood defenses fail to provide safety, have adverse effects on natural retention space for water, create a false public sense of security, and are costly to build. The new approach introduced a wider range of ﬂood management measures: land-use management/ planning; ﬂood insurance; ﬂood risk communication; environmental policies, such as preserving wetlands – along with ﬂood defenses. These measures aim at “making space for water/rivers,” as well as recognizing the difﬁculty of fully controlling ﬂooding and the shortcomings of ﬂood defenses. The concept and instruments of risk form an important element in many of these initiatives and policy reforms. Notably, the EU Flood Directive (EC 2007) treats risk assessments as important for the efﬁcient allocation of resources and explicitly deﬁning the boundaries of ﬂood management. Concretely, risk assessments provide a valuable basis for priority setting and further technical, ﬁnancial and political deci- sions regarding ﬂood risk management. (EC 2007, para. 12). In terms of more accountable governance, the Flood Directive requires member states to produce ﬂood risk maps and risk management plans and make them publicly accessible. Flood management in the two country cases, Germany and England,1 in general mirrors this paradigm shift. Germany and England have reviewed their approaches to Institutions and risk-based governance K. The reforms in the two countries adopted the labels “making space for water” (DEFRA 2005) and “room for rivers” (Bundesregierung 2005a). As the concept of room/space suggests, land-use regulation has become a central part of the emerging ﬂood manage- ment approaches (LAWA 1995, 2004; DCLG 2006). Other domains beyond ﬂood defenses, such as commercial insurance for private risk management (LAWA 2004; ABI & Government 2008) and ﬂood risk communication (EA 2006; UBA 2006), have also received attention. Flood managers in Germany and England also stress the central role played by risk assessments in the reorganization of ﬂood management, from deﬁning risk zones for land-use planning and ﬁnancial decisions for ﬂood defense measures, to enabling insur- ance companies to offer cover for ﬂood damage. The UK Environment Ministry, DEFRA promises that“risk evidence base will drive our risk management activities” (2005, p. 19), while Germany’s key ﬂood management guidance acknowledges that risk-assessment instruments and improved knowledge about the threats are essential for targeted, forward-looking ﬂood management (LAWA 2004). At ﬁrst glance, developments in ﬂood management, therefore, tell a story of the rise of similarly risk-based governance in areas in which resources are scarce and perfor- mance of governance has, in failing to keep the citizens’ feet dry, fallen short of public expectations. A closer look at ﬂood management in Germany and England, however, raises questions as to the universality and uniformity of risk-based governance: For instance, why does England’s central government strongly rely on a risk-based beneﬁt–cost ratio to allocate its ﬂood defense budget, while Germany’s federal govern- ment allocates most of its spending in accordance to the Königsberger Schlüssel, an allocation key proportional to the population numbers of Germany’s 16 states? Why do England’s national planning policies cover areas exposed to ﬂood events as seldom as once every 1,000 years with relatively ﬂexible regulations based on three risk zones, while Germany’s government regulates very restrictively, and almost exclu- sively, new property developments in areas that are inundated on average once every 100 years? These questions point to a diversity in risk-based ﬂood management that seems inconsistent with the apparent international convergence in ﬂood manage- ment and scholarly arguments about the universal and uniform rise of risk-based governance. As this paper will empirically demonstrate, the conceptualization and use of risk in governance can display signiﬁcant variation between countries. The argument of this paper is that risk-based governance is shaped by a set of country-speciﬁc institutional variables. Speciﬁcally, the paper shows how administrative and state structures, proce- dures, norms, as well as political expectations, promote, ﬁlter, or constrain the adoption of risk-based governance. This paper is organized as follows: Section 2 brieﬂy discusses the universal appeal and drivers of risk-based governance, and contrasts these with neo-institutionalist arguments that explain cross-country variance in regulation and public policy. Section 3 produces a snapshot of ﬂood management practices in Germany and England and the role of risk. Section 4 identiﬁes three sets of institutional variables and demonstrates how they result in substantial variation in the use of risk in ﬂood management. The concluding section 5 relates the empirical ﬁndings to the arguments from section 1 and sketches out a Institutions and risk-based governance K. Risk-based governance everywhere? Risk is generally taken to mean a combination of probability and adverse impact (Hood et al. 2004). This deﬁnition includes three core aspects: quantiﬁcation and science-based assessments (“objectively” calculating risk); monetarization of adverse consequences (expressing probable damage in currency units as comparable and widely comprehen- sible terms); and the use of probability theory to reduce uncertainty as much as possible (assessing the probability of damage occurring). These aspects of risk resonate with two main arguments in the debate of its governing. The ﬁrst argument is concerned with an economic-efﬁciency orientation in state activities, as well as with the role of the state in society and economy. These arguments are often associated with critical accounts of neoliberalism. Neoliberal arguments accuse the post-war state of inefﬁciency and promote market-based and economic efﬁciency- oriented governance (Peck & Tickell 2002; Castree 2008a,b). Speciﬁcally, a neoliberal perspective understands risk-based governance as a means to address problems of over- regulation (Black 2005; Hutter 2005), diffuse an economic logic across an ever-increasing number of areas of government and society (O’Malley 2004), and introduce non-state solutions to risk management challenges (Ericson et al. 2003; O’Malley 2003). Quantiﬁcation and monetarization enable an economic evaluation of state inter- ventions via cost–beneﬁt analysis and economic impact assessment. At the same time, quantifying and monetarizing probable damage is an important prerequisite for the mobilization of insurance markets (Priest 1996; Swiss Re 2002). Furthermore, informing individuals of their exposure to threats on the basis of intelligible risk terms enables and “responsibilizes” individuals to manage their own risks (Ericson et al. 2003). The second argument is about accountability pressures on state actors. These require actors to explain and justify their decisions and performance vis-à-vis other parties. Traditional mechanisms of accountability (such as Parliament, courts, and auditors) have been complemented and reinforced by other developments: One side of this is societal (e.g. the less deferential citizens of late modernity [Giddens 1991]) and technological change (e.g. Internet access) that increases public scrutiny of state interventions. The other side includes legal (e.g. freedom of information legislation) and regulatory changes (e.g. the rise of regulation inside government [Light 1993; Hood et al. 1999]). The increased public scrutiny and accountability pressures have two implications. First, actors increasingly have reporting obligations and are subject to auditing. Risk instruments quantify, standardize, and monetarize the potential harmful consequences. This allows for the quantitative assessment of the status quo, and of the effectiveness of interventions, as well as the deﬁnition of the objectives of state activities, in turn facili- tating the assessment of those activities by the public and other auditors. Second, risk instruments can help state actors better deal with public scrutiny and accountability pressures that intensify blame-attribution games, and, thus, the actors’ blame-deﬂection efforts. Blame games occur as a consequence of governance failures (especially those concerning health and safety risks [Hood 2002; Rothstein et al. 2006a]), which can be argued to be inevitable in view of the bounded rationality in decisionmaking (Simon 1957). Risk-based governance may serve defensive purposes by contributing to the Institutions and risk-based governance K. Krieger © 2012 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 239 Page 5: blame-deﬂection capacities of actors. This is, ﬁrst, because risk-based governance assigns special credibility and legitimacy to its users and their risk-based interventions because of its quantitative and science-based, thus,“objective” character (Miller & Rose 1990; Porter 1995). Second, if actors use risk instruments as an informational basis, the fact that risk only provides the odds (and not certainty) offers a justiﬁcation should anything go wrong (Luhmann 1993; Rothstein et al. 2006a). Both neoliberalization and rising accountability pressures have been presented as universal trends, underpinned by the wide range of cross-country and cross-sectoral neoliberalization case studies (Castree 2008a) and cross-country political commitment to accountability and good governance (EC 2001). In relation to risk-based governance, this would imply that risk-based governance (revolving around quantiﬁcation, scientiﬁc foundations, monetarization/calculations of economic damage, and the presence of uncertainty) can also be expected to be a universal phenomenon. However, there are reasons to question this universality in the adoption of this particular form of risk-based governance. First, scholars have questioned the universality of neoliberalization and accountability pressures. On the former, Castree (2008b), for instance, advises that substantive commonalities of neoliberalization cases may some- times be so limited that neoliberalization in a particular case only exists conceptually, but not in actuality. On the latter, Hood and Scott (2000), in their analysis of regulation inside government, highlight the lack of systematic, cross-national surveys of regulation. Second, Rothstein et al. (2013) have argued that the adoption of risk-based governance is shaped by differences between countries regarding norms and accountability structures. Similarly, literature in the closely related ﬁelds of environmental governance and the politics of (risk) regulation highlight variations between state regulations, at times even in the supranationally regulated policy ﬁelds of the European Union (Jasanoff 1986;Vogel 1986; Schrader-Frechette 1991; Knill & Lenschow 1998; Héritier et al. 2001; Hood et al. 2004; Burgess 2006; Lees 2007; Lodge et al. 2008). These differences are frequently explained with reference to institutional variables. Institutional variables in these studies include the separation of powers between executive and legislative, and low barriers to litigation (Vogel 1983); standard operating procedures in public administration and fragmentation of the state (Lees 2007); prevailing world views according to cultural theory (Lodge et al. 2008); and political culture (Jasanoff 1986, 2005). These examples show how institutionalized policy ideas, structures, and approaches prevail vis-a-vis or shape emerging ideas, such as risk-based governance, because of the path dependency of policymaking and organizational change (Krasner 1983; North 1990; Pierson 2000) or the logic of appropriateness and institutional ﬁt with existing arrangements (March & Olsen 1984; Knill 1999). Is there a convergence between countries toward uniform risk-based governance? Or can we identify institutionally determined variance in the uses of risk in governance? In response to these questions, the following sections develop and apply a cross-country comparative analysis of ﬂood management in Germany and England in order to explore the effects of institutional variables on the use of risk in governance. 3. The description and explanation are informed by an extensive analysis of key policy documents (for insights on policy and institutional context, goals and standards, conceptualizations of risk, methods of risk assessment, rationale/purpose of the use of risk, and challenges to risk-based approach), as well as 48 semi-structured interviews with key decisionmakers and stakeholders in the ﬂood management of Germany and England (to understand the actors’ motivations and constraints for certain policy choices and complement the ﬁndings from the document analysis). Documents and interview data were collected from organizations of water, environment, and planning authorities at different levels of government, as well as stakeholders (including insurers, interest groups, such as environmental NGOs, farmers, and property developers) (see Appendix). 3.1. Snapshot of ﬂood management in Germany and England This section provides a snapshot of ﬂood-defense management and land-use regulation within Germany and England’s ﬂood management. It describes the regulatory core of the two policy domains (along with their use of ﬁnancial and informational resources to implement the regulations), as they have emerged between the 1990s and 2007/8.2 Flood defense is the classical arena for ﬂood control. Based on engineering, it involves the construction of ﬁxed defenses to prevent ﬂooding. As noted, while ﬂood defense engineering has undergone some changes since the 1980s (e.g. defense realignment to restore water retention on natural ﬂoodplains), the aim in this policy domain is the reduction, if not elimination, of the occurrence of frequent and damaging ﬂood events. Land-use regulation, primarily restrictions on the use of areas at risk of inundation to remove people and property from harm’s way, has emerged more recently as a key response to ﬂooding. Land-use regulation can address the problem of asset accumulation in areas at risk (including areas deemed safe behind ﬂood defenses), does not require public infrastructure spending, and has beneﬁcial environmental effects. However, restrictions associated with such regulations negatively affect economic activities in areas at risk. Given cost pressures and the threat of over-regulation, risk-based approaches in the two domains emerge as appealing policy options. Risk features in each of the two domains across both countries, but in distinctive ways and to varying degrees. 3.2. Flood defense management What does risk-based ﬂood defense management look like in the two countries? Assess- ments of risk can be argued to help steer public spending and safety standards for ﬂood defenses to where the probable damage is expected to be highest. With speciﬁc regard to ﬂood defense management, three distinctions can be identiﬁed in the use of risk between the two countries. The ﬁrst distinction concerns the disparate types of policy objectives set for ﬂood defense management. Germany’s state aims at providing similar levels of safety to its population through the HQ standards (with HQ being the acronym for water level), in particular HQ100. The HQ100 standard, in the context of ﬂood defense management, provides for properties to be protected against any event that statistically occurs once or more every 100 years. If an event occurs that is statistically less frequent, say once in 150 years (HQ150), individuals need to be prepared to be inundated. While HQ100 is not the only HQ standard and is not formally deﬁned as the minimum safety standard in all Institutions and risk-based governance K. Krieger © 2012 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 241 Page 7: German states, its central role in ﬂood management manifests itself in a number of ways – for instance, as the objective for the ﬂood management plans developed by the states as required under the Federal Flood Protection Act from 2005 (Berendes 2005); as a key standard and policy objective for ﬂood defense managers in Saxony (Socher et al. 2006); as a condition for obtaining state funding for local ﬂood defense projects in North Rhine-Westphalia (Krieger 2012), or as a minimal protection standard required by administrative court decisions in Bavaria (LAWA 2004). England’s state, in contrast, deﬁnes targets for the economic and risk management performance of state activities, but the targets do not commit the state to providing a particular level of protection to the population. DEFRA introduced so-called Outcome Measures in 2007 (DEFRA 2007), a country-level measurement which replaced a system strongly shaped by project-level cost–beneﬁt assessments, complemented by generic guidance on funding priorities and indicative ﬂood defense standards (MAFF 1993; Scrase & Sheate 2005). Two of the Outcome Measures are relevant for ﬂood defense management and the associated use of funding: Outcome Measure 1 (OM-1) establishes a minimal aggregate cost–beneﬁt ratio of 5:1 for national public spending on ﬂood management (whereby the beneﬁt is calculated in avoided risk [probable damage]). Outcome Measure 2 (OM-2) stipulates that ﬂood defense management is to aim at moving a certain number of properties (140,000 between 2008 and 2011) from relatively higher to lower risk categories (identiﬁed in DEFRA’s National Flood Risk Assessment). The second distinction concerns the different conceptualization of risk: speciﬁcally, whether both probability and consequence are taken into account in ﬂood defense management. Germany’s HQ standard is a purely probabilistic standard that does not take into account potential economic damage. England’s OMs are fully risk-based objec- tives, taking into account properties at risk and avoided probable damage. The third distinction is about the centrality of risk in determining the allocation of ﬁnancial resources for ﬂood-defense spending. Some of Germany’s funding is not shaped by the HQ100 standard, but by allocation principles that do not consider hazard and risk calculations at all. At state level, ﬁnancial resources are indeed normally geared towards achieving the probabilistic HQ standards. However, the federal government – in conjunc- tion with states – provides funds that follow different allocational principles: The Joint Task Agriculture and Coastal Protection (GAK) uses the Königsberger Schlüssel, which allocates funding in accordance to population numbers. Financial aid to the states affected by ﬂooding in 1997 (Brandenburg) and 2002 (Saxony; Saxony-Anhalt) was mobilized on an ad hoc politically negotiated basis immediately after the disasters (Schwarze & Wagner 2006). In England, risk-based OMs determine the allocation of almost all funding available for ﬂood management. This is because funding has – follow- ing the criticism of the previous, mixed local–central funding in the aftermath of the Easter ﬂoods in 1998 (SCA 1998) – been increasingly centralized and, thus, become subject to central government control. 3.3. Land-use regulation What does risk-based land-use regulation look like in Germany and England? Assess- ments of risk can be argued to allow for varying the restrictiveness of land-use regulation in accordance to risk levels, that is, the probability of inundation and the vulnerability of the planned land use. Such a risk-based targeting of regulation can also help avoid the risk Institutions and risk-based governance K. Krieger © 2012 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 242 Page 8: of over-regulation. Three differences can be observed in the use of risk in land-use regulation in Germany and England. The ﬁrst difference lies in the rigidity and spatial coverage of risk-based regulation of land use. In Germany, the HQ100 reference standard (stipulated in the 2005 Federal Flood Protection Act) identiﬁes the tightly regulated inundation areas (“Überschwem- mungsgebiete”) (Berendes 2005; Bundesregierung 2005b). As a result, planners and developers who want to economically utilize an area that is likely to be ﬂooded more frequently than once every 100 years face a de facto ban on development plans. Beyond these areas, federal legislation deﬁnes the ﬂood-prone areas (“überschwemmungsgefähr- dete Gebiete”), but deﬁnes these areas vaguely as areas in which inundation results in adverse effects on public well-being (“Wohl der Allgemeinheit”), and does not declare them as actual inundation areas. England’s regulations cover the area that would be inundated in events with a sta- tistical return period of once every 1,000 years. This area is split into three ﬂood zones, displayed on the Environment Agency’s Flood Map as natural ﬂoodplain (>HQ20); high risk (>HQ100); and moderate risk (HQ1,000). The regulation of this large area is ﬂexible and differentiated: Local planners are advised, using a procedure called the Sequential Test, to steer development toward the low-risk ﬂood zone (DETR 2001; DCLG 2006). In addition to this general rule, however, planners may take into account the vulnerability of a development (via Vulnerability Classiﬁcation) and its wider sustainability beneﬁts (via Exception Test). In other words, nurseries, for instance, can be treated differently from retailers (because of their varying vulnerability levels), and commercial areas from residential areas (because of differences in economic beneﬁts). The second difference concerns the design of the risk-assessment instrument used in land-use regulation, the ﬂood map. Germany’s ﬂood maps display the protective effects of ﬂood defenses. In England, the ﬂood map of the Environment Agency (EA) displays the extent of inundation, while disregarding the effects of ﬂood defenses. This disparity points to a different treatment of the uncertainty of the protection provided by ﬂood defenses. The third difference concerns the effectiveness of risk-based regulation. In Germany, the binary pattern of regulation (tightly regulated “inundation areas” vs. vaguely regu- lated “ﬂood-prone” areas) is echoed in the implementation of the regulations. The legal concept of “inundation areas” in the Federal Flood Protection Act of 2005 is an imme- diately effective regulation that is directly binding for the local planning ofﬁcers. A ﬂood manager notes that the only thing that we can imagine and see in practice [in HQ100 areas] are shipyards and ports. (German Regional Water Authority 2009, interview) In fact, a local planner describes his position as being “cornered” by the regulations. For “ﬂood-prone” areas, the states are required to ﬁll out the details of the vague concept. The states in turn – if they adopt“ﬂood-prone”areas at all – regulate the areas very weakly (e.g. North-Rhine Westphalia’s generic regulation to reduce adverse consequences to the public good or Saxony-Anhalt’s purely informational requirement to make a note in planning decisions that a property is in a ﬂood-prone area) (Krieger 2012). In England, lack of compliance of local planning authorities (LPAs) with national planning policies has emerged as a major problem for achieving risk reduction goals, such Institutions and risk-based governance K. Krieger © 2012 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 243 Page 9: as the LPAs’ authorization of development in ﬂood risk areas. This has been argued to reﬂect the non-binding character of national planning policies, as well as conﬂicting local priorities (Howe & White 2002, 2004). However, compliance has improved substantially over recent years, from 40 percent of planning proposals being approved against the advice of the EA based on the EA’s risk assessments in 2001/2002, to only 10 percent in 2007/2008 (EA 2004, 2008). The use of risk in ﬂood-defense management and in land-use regulation in the two countries varies in a number of fundamental aspects, from the ways in which risk is conceptualized to the centrality, scope, purpose, and form of its application. The follow- ing section explains the limits and variety in risk-based ﬂood management from an institutionalist perspective. 4. Institutional variables and their effects on risk-based ﬂood management Three sets of institutional variables shape the use of risk in ﬂood management in Germany and England: political culture and norms; style of public administration; and state structure. As the following sections show, these variables offer explanations for the differences in the two countries’ risk-based ﬂood management discussed in the previous section. 4.1. Normative foundations of the state and risk-based ﬂood management The ﬁrst variable that affects the use of risk in governance is normative. It concerns the perceptions of statehood, its objectives and responsibilities, as they are held by key actors within the ﬂood management approaches, and as they manifest themselves in policy initiatives and normative foundations of polities. The normative foundations of Germany’s statehood deﬁne it as a protective state. The German constitution, the Grundgesetz or Basic Law, offers an instructive starting point to learn about these foundations of Germany’s polity and ﬂood management. This is because, as Dyson notes, Germany’s constitutional norms are not only seen “as a general framework establishing a minimum consensus about certain principles,” but “as a politi- cal programme containing particular substantive goals”(Dyson 1980, p. 213). This politi- cal program ascribes a signiﬁcant responsibility to the state for the well-being and protection of the population, whether through the core norm concerning the protection of human dignity (article 1) and physical integrity (article 2) or the welfare-state article (article 28). How do these normative foundations shape Germany’s risk-based ﬂood manage- ment? Perceptions of a protective state are reﬂected in many ways in the ﬂood manage- ment: Interviewed state and local ﬂood managers speak about their role in the Daseinsvorsorge (protection from mishaps) of individuals; policy guidance deﬁnes effec- tive ﬂood management as a “highest-order” public good of “overriding signiﬁcance” (Berendes 2005, p. 202), linked to the well-being of the population in general (Wohl der Allgemeinheit) (LAWA 1995, p. 2), while administrative court cases stressing the state’s duty to provide protection to residential and working areas suggest a concrete relevance of constitutional norms to the ﬂood management context (LAWA 2004). The ﬂipside of the wide-ranging state responsibility is the perception, in the words of a local ﬂood manager, that Institutions and risk-based governance K.
